one_line_takeaway: A literature-review-plus-design-science paper that catalogs the security and epistemic failure modes of agent-only social networks (OpenClaw/Moltbook) and proposes two governed multi-agent scientific-research platforms - ClawdLab and beach.science - whose tool-verified evidence and role-restricted governance are argued to neutralise Sybil/karma manipulation, but which remain architecturally validated only, not empirically tested.
key_finding: The paper's central design claim is that the epistemic failures of karma-based agent social networks can be structurally engineered away by substituting external tool-verified evidence and hard role-restricted governance for social consensus. Concretely, it argues ClawdLab converts Sybil attacks from a quality-signal threat into mere throughput (more agents = more scouts/analysts/critics, none able to trigger votes), collapsing the attack surface to a single accountable, publicly-auditable PI. Its honest empirical finding, however, is negative/limited: ClawdLab is operational only with the developer team's own agents with no external-agent testing and no longitudinal data; beach.science has produced only prototype interactions with the reward/quality-gate system unbuilt, and in one illustrative case the agent still required intermittent human operator steering to complete a multi-step workflow, exposing a gap between the autonomous-discovery vision and operational reality.
core_contribution: This is a combined multivocal literature review (MLR) plus design-science paper, not an empirical or benchmark study. Its own contributions are four: (1) a synthesis of the OpenClaw autonomous-agent framework and the Moltbook agent-only social network drawing on six early preprints and a body of gray literature; (2) a catalogue of five recurring architectural patterns across that ecosystem (community-maintained skill registry, persistent agent identity, emergent collective behavior within structured spaces, periodic heartbeat re-engagement, and exclusively social/karma-based content evaluation); (3) ClawdLab, an authored open-source platform (the paper's designed artifact) in which AI agents self-organize into research labs under hard role restrictions, structured adversarial critique, PI-led quorum voting, multi-model role orchestration, and evidence that must be verified through external tool/API/MCP invocations rather than social consensus; and (4) beach.science, a companion free-form public research commons using template-based roles, extensible skill registries, and a planned programmatic inference-credit reward system. A three-tier taxonomy (single-agent pipelines; predetermined multi-agent workflows; fully decentralised systems) frames why existing AI co-scientist systems remain in the first two tiers, with ClawdLab/beach.science positioned as a composable third-tier design. CRITICAL SEPARATION: the widely quoted numbers about Moltbook (prompt-injection counts, toxicity rates, agent inflation, the CVE) are findings of the SIX REVIEWED STUDIES and third parties, not measurements produced by this paper.
threat_model: The paper is anchored in a real agentic-AI threat model that it draws from the reviewed ecosystem rather than inventing. On the attack-surface side (documented by cited work it reviews): community skill registries create software-supply-chain risk (Wang et al.'s PASBench found vulnerabilities across 131 skills at prompt-processing, external-content, tool-invocation, and memory-retrieval stages); Moltbook exhibited 506 prompt-injection attacks and a Sybil/agent-inflation problem (1.5M registered agents traced to ~17,000 human owners, 88:1, due to absent rate limits per Wiz/Nagli); and SecurityScorecard reported CVE-2026-25253 (CVSS 8.8, one-click RCE via auth-token exfiltration) plus ~15,200 OpenClaw panels exposed by default 0.0.0.0 binding. On the DEFENSE side, which IS the paper's own contribution, ClawdLab is argued to structurally neutralise several of these: (a) Sybil/vote-flooding resistance as an emergent property, since extra agents can only act within role-restricted task types, no specialist role can initiate voting, and only a PI can advance tasks constrained to attached computational evidence, reducing the residual attack surface to a single 'malicious/negligent PI' (a trust-in-leadership problem) mitigated by public audit logs; (b) tool-grounded 'definition of done' replacing manipulation-prone karma. Planned (not-yet-implemented) defenses: Ed25519 claim signing with signature chains for tamper-evident provenance, canary tokens for plagiarism detection, prompt-injection sanitisation middleware, and anomaly detection over submission frequency / domain switching / vote clustering.
limitations: The authors are candid. (1) The reviewed evidence base is temporally shallow: all six studies observed a platform 0-14 days old (longest ~5-day window), cannot separate transient novelty effects from stable patterns, and suffer dataset overlap (the Moltbook Observatory Archive feeds at least two studies, giving apparent corroboration from shared samples rather than independent replication). (2) Reviewed findings are methodologically heterogeneous and not commensurable (lexicon sentiment without manual validation vs LLM-assisted thematic coding); observed 'norm enforcement' may just be RLHF training-time safety patterns, and network 'community formation' may be intra-operator coordination given the 88:1 inflation. (3) The paper's OWN artifacts are largely unvalidated: ClawdLab's incentive structures are untested with external agents, the claimed Nash equilibrium favouring diverse team composition is unverified, and beach.science's core reward system and quality gates are not yet deployed - findings should be read as 'design commitments' pending future empirical work. (4) Several security defenses are planned/aspirational, not implemented. (5) Note for graph consumers: this paper is one degree removed from the primary security data - it reports rather than produces the CVE, injection counts, and toxicity metrics.

datasets_used: The paper's own 'data' is its review corpus: six formal preprints/reports on the OpenClaw-Moltbook ecosystem (Riegler & Gautam 2026; Manik & Wang 2026; Lin et al. 2026; Jiang et al. 2026; Eziz 2026; Wang et al. 2026) plus gray literature (GitHub repos, developer interview in The Pragmatic Engineer, journalism, CACM commentary)
Reviewed (not collected by this paper) - Moltbook Observatory Archive on HuggingFace (Gautam & Riegler 2026), the primary dataset for at least two of the reviewed studies

Reviewed corpora sizes reported by cited studies: 19,802 posts + 2,812 comments (Riegler & Gautam); 39,026 posts + 5,712 comments from 14,490 agents (Manik & Wang); 44,411 posts + 12,209 submolts (Jiang et al.); 12,758 submolts (Lin et al.)
Beach.science live platform metrics (the paper's own artifact, Figure 6): 52 science agents, 924 hypotheses, 2,206 comments, 50 discussions
quantitative_results: PAPER'S OWN ARTIFACT (ClawdLab config): 5 role archetypes (PI, research analyst, scout, critic, synthesizer); quorum rule = minimum 2 substantive votes from at least half the lab's active membership, strict majority decides; agent pull loop polls every 45-90 seconds; heartbeat required at least every 5 minutes; 4 conclusion types (proven, disproven, pivoted, inconclusive); analysis provider proxy uses SHA-256 dataset checksums

PAPER'S OWN ARTIFACT (ClawdLab demo): traced Protein Annotation Sanity Checker lab; PI decomposed the question into 3 initial literature-review tasks, later 5; synthesis completed within a ~5-minute window without human intervention

REVIEWED/THIRD-PARTY (not this paper's measurements): OpenClaw ~179,000 GitHub stars and 29,600 forks; ClawHub hosted 5,705+ community skills; connects to 15+ messaging platforms; Moltbook claimed 1.5M agents in 72h but Wiz found only ~17,000 human owners (88:1 ratio); Riegler & Gautam found 506 prompt-injection attacks and 19.3% cryptocurrency content; Manik & Wang found 18.4% of posts action-inducing; Jiang et al. found technology content 93.11% safe vs political 39.74% safe, with harmful content surging to 66.71% at peak; Wang et al. (PASBench) tested 131 threatening skills; CVE-2026-25253 scored CVSS 8.8; ~15,200 exposed OpenClaw panels
baselines_compared: No empirical baselines - the paper runs no comparative experiments. Comparisons are architectural contrasts: ClawdLab/beach.science tool-verified evidence and role-restricted governance are contrasted against Moltbook's karma-based social-consensus evaluation (which cited studies showed elevated crypto promotions and anti-human manifestos)
ClawdLab's decentralised pull model is contrasted with the centralised world-state coordination of the Deep Research multi-agent system (Weidener et al. 2026)

The three-tier taxonomy positions existing AI co-scientist systems (Coscientist/Boiko et al. 2023; The AI Scientist/Lu et al. 2024; SciAgents; AI Scientist-v2/Yamada et al. 2025) in tiers 1-2 versus the proposed tier-3 decentralised architecture
